label: Maurya Empire
pitch:
Ancient India's first great empire (322-185 BCE), the Maurya dynasty unified the subcontinent for the first time. Chandragupta Founds Empire by overthrowing the Nanda dynasty, guided by the brilliant strategist Chanakya, Royal Advisor. His grandson Ashoka Becomes Emperor and conquers Kalinga in a war so bloody it haunts him — he converts to Buddhism and spends the rest of his reign carving Ashoka's Rock Edicts that still stand across India today.

🔑 Key Facts:
• Arthashastra Composed by Chanakya is an ancient masterclass in statecraft, economics, and espionage — sometimes called 'India's Machiavelli' (1,800 years before Machiavelli)
• The Kalinga War was so devastating that Ashoka Converts to Buddhism and renounced violence, transforming from conqueror to peacemaker
• The Lion Capital of Ashoka at Sarnath became modern India's national emblem — you see it on every Indian rupee
• The Great Stupa at Sanchi, commissioned by Ashoka, is one of the oldest stone structures in India

🤔 Why It Matters: Ashoka's Buddhist Missions Abroad transformed Buddhism from a regional Indian sect into a world religion now practiced by 500+ million people. The Maurya model of centralized governance influenced Indian politics for millennia.

🎲 Wild Card: Chandragupta Embraces Jainism at the end of his life — the emperor who conquered an empire voluntarily gave it up and reportedly fasted to death as a Jain monk.

spans.label: Chandragupta Era
spans.description: Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the Nanda dynasty and unified most of the Indian subcontinent for the first time, guided by the strategist Chanakya. He defeated Seleucus Nicator, established Pataliputra as his grand capital, and later abdicated to become a Jain monk.

spans.label: Bindusara Era
spans.description: Bindusara expanded the empire southward across the Deccan plateau, earning the title Amitraghata (Slayer of Foes). He maintained diplomatic ties with the Hellenistic world and consolidated the administrative systems his father had built.

spans.label: Ashoka's Reign
spans.description: After the devastating Kalinga War, Ashoka converted to Buddhism and devoted his reign to promoting dharma, nonviolence, and welfare through inscribed rock and pillar edicts. He sent Buddhist missionaries abroad, transforming Buddhism into a world religion.

spans.label: Late Maurya Period
spans.description: Weak successors and ambitious provincial governors led to rapid fragmentation of the empire after Ashoka's death. Greco-Bactrian forces invaded the northwest, and General Pushyamitra Shunga assassinated the last Maurya emperor in 185 BCE.

spans.label: Nanda Dynasty
spans.description: The Nandas controlled Magadha and much of northern India, amassing great wealth and maintaining a massive army. Their unpopularity created the opening that Chandragupta and Chanakya exploited to seize power and found the Maurya Empire.

events.label: Nanda Dynasty Rules
events.description: The Nanda dynasty controlled Magadha and much of northern India, amassing great wealth but provoking discontent that Chandragupta would exploit.

events.label: Dhana Nanda, Last Nanda King
events.description: Dhana Nanda was the last ruler of the Nanda dynasty, whose heavy taxation and unpopularity created the conditions for Chandragupta's revolution. His massive army reportedly terrified Alexander's troops into mutiny.

events.label: Chandragupta Founds Empire
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the Nanda dynasty and established the Maurya Empire, unifying most of the Indian subcontinent for the first time.

events.label: Chandragupta Conquers Northwest
events.description: Chandragupta swept through the Punjab and Indus valley, liberating or conquering the Greek garrisons that Alexander of Macedon had left behind after his retreat in 326 BCE.

events.label: Defeat of Seleucus
events.description: Chandragupta defeated Seleucus Nicator in the Seleucid-Mauryan war, gaining territories in Afghanistan and Balochistan and sending 500 war elephants that reshaped the Hellenistic world.

events.label: Seleucid Marriage Alliance
events.description: The peace treaty between Chandragupta and Seleucus included a marriage alliance, probably a Seleucid princess married into the Maurya court, establishing sustained diplomatic contact between South Asia and the Mediterranean.

events.label: Maurya Elephants at Ipsus
events.description: The 500 war elephants Chandragupta gave Seleucus played a decisive role at the Battle of Ipsus, one of the key battles in the Wars of the Diadochi, reshaping the Hellenistic political map.

events.label: Arthashastra Composed
events.description: Kautilya (Chanakya) composed the Arthashastra, an ancient Indian treatise on statecraft, economics, espionage, and military strategy — one of the most comprehensive works on power ever written.

events.label: Chanakya, Royal Advisor
events.description: The philosopher Chanakya served as chief advisor to Chandragupta, masterminding the empire's founding and shaping Indian political thought for centuries. His three names (Chanakya, Kautilya, Vishnugupta) reflect how multiple literary traditions claimed him.

events.label: Pataliputra as Capital
events.description: Pataliputra became the grand Maurya capital, described by Megasthenes as roughly 9 miles long, surrounded by a timber palisade with 570 towers and 64 gates, and protected by a wide moat.

events.label: Megasthenes Visits India
events.description: Greek ambassador Megasthenes visited Pataliputra and wrote the Indica, providing one of the earliest foreign accounts of Indian society, describing the city's royal palace with gilded pillars and ornamental lakes.

events.label: Indica by Megasthenes
events.description: Megasthenes' Indica described Maurya society in detail, surviving through quotations by Strabo, Arrian, and Diodorus Siculus. It became the key source for Hellenistic knowledge of India.

events.label: Chandragupta Embraces Jainism
events.description: Chandragupta abdicated the throne, became a Jain monk, and traveled to Shravanabelagola in Karnataka, where he reportedly performed santhara — voluntary fasting unto death.

events.label: Bindusara's Reign
events.description: Bindusara expanded the empire southward across the Deccan plateau, earning the title Amitraghata (Slayer of Foes). He maintained diplomatic relations with the Hellenistic world.

events.label: Deccan Campaign
events.description: Bindusara conquered most of the Deccan plateau, extending Maurya authority to within about 300 miles of India's southern tip. Only the Tamil kingdoms of the Cholas, Pandyas, and Cheras remained independent.

events.label: Deimachus, Greek Ambassador
events.description: Deimachus served as Greek ambassador from the Seleucid court to Pataliputra during Bindusara's reign, continuing the tradition of Hellenistic diplomatic missions begun under Chandragupta.

events.label: Bindusara Patronizes Ajivikas
events.description: Bindusara was a patron of the Ajivikas, an ancient determinist sect that rivaled Buddhism and Jainism. The Maurya dynasty's religious pluralism — Jainism, Ajivika, Buddhism — was a family trait.

events.label: Succession Crisis
events.description: Bindusara's death triggered a violent succession struggle among his many sons. Ashoka, a younger prince who had governed Ujjain, outmaneuvered and probably killed his elder brother Susima to seize the throne.

events.label: Ashoka Governs Ujjain
events.description: The young prince Ashoka was sent to govern Ujjain, capital of the western province of Avanti. The administrative and military experience he gained there proved decisive in the succession crisis.

events.label: Ashoka Becomes Emperor
events.description: Ashoka ascended the Maurya throne after a violent succession struggle and would become one of India's most consequential rulers, transforming from conqueror to advocate of dharma.

events.label: Kalinga War
events.description: Ashoka's brutal conquest of Kalinga caused massive casualties — his own inscription claims 100,000 killed and 150,000 deported — triggering his famous conversion to Buddhism and nonviolence.

events.label: Ashoka Converts to Buddhism
events.description: Horrified by the carnage at Kalinga, Ashoka embraced Buddhism as a lay devotee (upasaka) and adopted dhamma as his governing philosophy, attempting to replace conquest with moral persuasion.

events.label: Ashoka's Rock Edicts
events.description: Ashoka inscribed his Fourteen Major Rock Edicts across the empire in Prakrit, proclaiming principles of dharma, religious tolerance, and compassion — the first explicit articulation of religious tolerance as governance policy in world history.

events.label: Religious Tolerance Edict (XII)
events.description: Rock Edict XII declared that all religions should be honored and none condemned — the first surviving articulation of religious tolerance as a principle of governance in world history.

events.label: Animal Welfare Edicts
events.description: Ashoka banned animal sacrifice in Pataliputra, restricted killing of certain species, and established veterinary hospitals for both humans and animals — the earliest known state-sponsored healthcare covering animals.

events.label: Dhamma Mahamatras Appointed
events.description: Ashoka created a new class of officials called Dhamma Mahamatras tasked with promoting ethical behavior, mediating disputes, inspecting prisons, and monitoring the welfare of women, the elderly, and the poor.

events.label: Ashoka's Pillar Edicts
events.description: The seven Pillar Edicts were inscribed on polished sandstone pillars up to 50 feet high, transported hundreds of miles from the Chunar quarries. Their mirror-like Maurya polish still impresses modern engineers.

events.label: Lion Capital of Ashoka
events.description: The four-lion capital at Sarnath — four Asiatic lions seated back-to-back on a circular abacus with a 24-spoke dharmachakra — became modern India's national emblem in 1950.

events.label: Ashoka Chakra on Indian Flag
events.description: The 24-spoke Dharma Wheel (Ashoka Chakra) from the Lion Capital was placed at the center of India's national flag in 1947, connecting the modern republic to a 3rd-century-BCE emperor's moral vision.

events.label: Chunar Sandstone Pillars
events.description: At least thirty monolithic sandstone pillars were quarried from the Chunar deposits near Varanasi, shaped, polished to a glass-like sheen, and transported hundreds of miles to their final locations across the empire.

events.label: Third Buddhist Council
events.description: Ashoka convened the Third Buddhist Council at Pataliputra, presided over by Moggaliputta Tissa, to purify the sangha and codify the Pali Canon, shaping Theravada Buddhism.

events.label: Pali Canon Codified
events.description: The Third Buddhist Council established the contents of the Pali Canon through oral agreement. It would not be written down until the 1st century BCE in Sri Lanka, ending centuries of purely oral transmission.

events.label: Buddhist Missions Abroad
events.description: Ashoka sent Buddhist missionaries to Sri Lanka, Gandhara, Kashmir, Central Asia, and claimed to have sent missions to five Hellenistic kingdoms, transforming Buddhism from a regional sect into a world religion.

events.label: Mahinda Brings Buddhism to Sri Lanka
events.description: Ashoka's son Mahinda led a Buddhist mission to Sri Lanka, converting King Devanampiya Tissa at Anuradhapura and establishing the longest unbroken Buddhist tradition in the world.

events.label: Sanghamitta and the Bodhi Tree
events.description: Ashoka's daughter Sanghamitta brought a cutting from the sacred Bodhi tree at Bodh Gaya to Sri Lanka and established a community of Buddhist nuns. The descendant tree at Anuradhapura is reputedly the oldest documented tree in the world.

events.label: Gandhara School of Buddhist Art
events.description: The interaction between Buddhism and Hellenistic Greek culture in Gandhara produced the first artistic tradition to depict the Buddha in human form, using Greco-Roman sculptural techniques. Every Buddha statue worldwide descends from this fusion.

events.label: Great Stupa at Sanchi
events.description: Ashoka commissioned the original brick structure at Sanchi, later enlarged and encased in stone with elaborately carved gateways. The stupa's hemispherical shape represents the dome of heaven.

events.label: Royal Highway (Uttarapatha)
events.description: The Maurya royal highway ran roughly 1,200 miles from Pataliputra to Taxila and beyond, with rest houses, milestones, shade trees, and wells — a direct descendant of the Persian Royal Road model.

events.label: Indian Ocean Trade Networks
events.description: Maurya-era ports including Bharuch in Gujarat shipped spices, textiles, ivory, and gemstones to the Hellenistic world, laying the groundwork for the massive Indian Ocean trade of later centuries.

events.label: Maurya Punch-Marked Coins
events.description: The Maurya state standardized silver punch-marked coins (karshapana), stamped with symbols like sun, wheel, tree, and elephant, creating a uniform monetary system across the subcontinent for the first time.

events.label: Spice Trade Expansion
events.description: Maurya-era trade in pepper, cinnamon, and cardamom connected southern India to Mediterranean markets via overland and maritime routes, beginning the spice trade that would become enormously lucrative.

events.label: State Monopolies
events.description: The Arthashastra describes state control over mines, salt production, forest products, and weapons manufacturing — a proto-mixed economy with private enterprise supervised by specialized superintendents.

events.label: Taxila as Learning Center
events.description: Taxila flourished as a renowned center of learning under the Mauryas, attracting scholars studying medicine, law, military arts, and philosophy. Both Chanakya and students from across the subcontinent studied here.

events.label: Maurya-Era Hospitals
events.description: Ashoka established hospitals for both humans and animals throughout the empire and ordered the cultivation and distribution of medicinal herbs — the earliest known state-sponsored civilian healthcare system.

events.label: Sudarshana Lake and Irrigation
events.description: The Maurya state built Sudarshana Lake in Gujarat, an artificial reservoir that remained in use for over six hundred years. Inscriptions from the 2nd century BCE and 5th century CE both mention repairs to the dam.

events.label: Maurya Iron and Steel
events.description: Maurya-era smiths produced high-carbon steel through techniques that would eventually evolve into wootz steel, prized across the ancient world and later traded to produce the famous Damascus steel blades.

events.label: Maurya Legal System
events.description: The Arthashastra describes courts at village, district, and provincial levels with the king as final appeal. The system distinguished civil and criminal law, specifying rules of evidence and judicial procedure.

events.label: Maurya Spy Network
events.description: Kautilya's Arthashastra describes an elaborate surveillance apparatus with resident agents (samstha), roving agents (sancara) disguised as monks or merchants, poisoners, assassins, and agents provocateurs — all reporting independently to the king.

events.label: Brahmi Script Standardized
events.description: The Brahmi script became widely standardized across the Maurya Empire for Ashoka's inscriptions. It is the ancestor of virtually every script used in South and Southeast Asia today, used by billions of people.

events.label: Kharosthi Script
events.description: The Kharosthi script, derived from Aramaic and written right to left, was used in the northwestern provinces for Ashoka's edicts. Some edicts in the far northwest also used Greek and Aramaic.

events.label: Arthashastra Rediscovered
events.description: R. Shamasastry rediscovered a complete Arthashastra manuscript at the Mysore Oriental Library among palm-leaf manuscripts. Published in 1909, it stunned the scholarly world with its systematic scope.

events.label: Prinsep Deciphers Brahmi
events.description: British officer James Prinsep cracked the Brahmi script through painstaking comparison of coin legends and inscriptions, allowing Ashoka's edicts to be read for the first time in over a thousand years.

events.label: Nehru Adopts Ashoka's Symbols
events.description: India's first prime minister Jawaharlal Nehru championed Ashoka's Lion Capital as the national emblem and the Ashoka Chakra for the flag, claiming Ashoka as proof that India had always been capable of tolerant, moral governance.

events.label: Death of Ashoka
events.description: Ashoka died after nearly four decades of rule, leaving an empire held together by a moral vision that had exactly one indispensable component: Ashoka himself.

events.label: Dasharatha Maurya
events.description: Dasharatha succeeded Ashoka and continued patronizing Ajivika monks, dedicating the Barabar Caves rock-cut sanctuaries — continuing the family tradition of religious pluralism.

events.label: Empire Fragments
events.description: After Ashoka's death, weak successors and provincial governors led to rapid fragmentation, with outlying territories breaking away from central control within a generation.

events.label: Barabar Caves
events.description: The Barabar Caves in Bihar are the oldest surviving rock-cut caves in India, dedicated to Ajivika monks by Ashoka and Dasharatha. They feature the same mirror-smooth polished surfaces found on Ashoka's pillars.

events.label: Lumbini Pillar Inscription
events.description: Ashoka erected a pillar at Lumbini identifying it as the Buddha's birthplace. Rediscovered in 1896, this inscription is key evidence establishing Siddhartha Gautama as a real historical person.

events.label: Pushyamitra's Coup
events.description: General Pushyamitra Shunga assassinated the last Maurya emperor Brihadratha during a military parade, ending the Maurya dynasty and founding the Shunga dynasty.

events.label: Shunga Dynasty Founded
events.description: Pushyamitra founded the Shunga dynasty, associated with a Brahmanical revival and a shift away from Ashoka's non-sectarian dhamma back toward Vedic orthodoxy.

events.label: Greek Incursions in Northwest
events.description: Greco-Bactrian king Demetrius I invaded the weakening northwestern Maurya territories, eventually establishing Indo-Greek kingdoms in the Punjab — sophisticated Hellenistic rulers who minted bilingual coins and patronized Buddhist art.

events.label: Centralized Bureaucracy
events.description: The Maurya Empire developed a sophisticated administrative system with four main provinces, each governed by a viceroy, subdivided into districts and villages with appointed officials and spies.

events.label: Jain Communities Flourish
events.description: Jainism thrived under Maurya patronage, with Chandragupta's conversion lending royal prestige and communities spreading across western and southern India.

events.label: Ashoka Visits Bodh Gaya
events.description: Ashoka visited and patronized the key Buddhist pilgrimage sites including Bodh Gaya (the Buddha's enlightenment), Sarnath (first sermon), Lumbini (birth), and Kushinagar (death).

events.label: Maurya Stone-Polishing Technique
events.description: Maurya craftsmen developed a stone-finishing technique producing glass-like surfaces that survived over 2,200 years. The method, possibly involving silica-based burnishing, was never replicated in later Indian stone-working.

events.label: Ashokavadana Legend
events.description: The Ashokavadana (Legend of Ashoka) shaped the Buddhist memory of the emperor, painting his pre-conversion self as Chandashoka (Ashoka the Fierce) to heighten the contrast with his later transformation.

events.label: Greek Historians Preserve Maurya Records
events.description: Strabo, Arrian, and Diodorus Siculus preserved fragments of Megasthenes' lost Indica in their own works, becoming essential sources for reconstructing the Maurya period.

events.label: Moggaliputta Tissa Presides
events.description: The monk Moggaliputta Tissa presided over the Third Buddhist Council at Pataliputra and organized the nine missionary expeditions that carried Buddhism beyond India.

events.label: Brihadratha, Last Maurya Emperor
events.description: Brihadratha was the last Maurya emperor, so weak that his own general Pushyamitra could murder him during a military review in front of troops who did nothing to stop it.
